title: An amparo success, though not yet complete

Red-tagging as a form of harassment that can trigger amparo because the Red-tagged person becomes a target of vigilantes, paramilitary groups, or state agents

Writ vs privilege distinction analogous to (but not identical to) preliminary vs permanent injunction in civil cases

Retired Justice Adolfo S. Azcuna (1991 bar examiner; 'Father of the Writ of Amparo')

stances.claim: The writ of amparo issuance in Deduro v. Vinoya is a 'victory of activists' (journalists, students, laborers, jeepney drivers, plain citizens) exercising freedom of expression via rallies, writings, speeches, and body language â€” but it is only a 'good start' because Deduro must still produce substantial evidence at the summary proceedings to obtain the actual 'privilege of the writ.'
stances.rhetorical_move: celebrate-but-qualify framing distinguishing writ from privilege

stances.claim: Retired Justice Adolfo S. Azcuna deserves the title 'Father of the Writ of Amparo in the Philippines' for his untiring spade work â€” proposing it as a 1971 ConCon delegate (failing because few understood), trying again at the 1986 ConCom (where CJ Concepcion redirected it to SC powers from the Bill of Rights), and crystallizing the concept first encountered in Prof. Fix-Zamudio's Mexican exposition.

The 'writ of amparo' (ex parte issuance on prima facie face-of-petition showing) must be carefully distinguished from the 'privilege of the writ of amparo' (granted after summary hearings on substantial evidence), analogous to but not identical with preliminary injunction (ex parte) vs permanent injunction (post-trial) in civil cases â€” with the evidentiary scale running prima facie â†’ substantial evidence (amparo) vs preponderance (civil) vs proof beyond reasonable doubt (criminal).

Red-tagging is a form of harassment that constitutes a cognizable cause to invoke the writ of amparo because the Red-tagged person becomes a target of vigilantes, paramilitary groups, or even state agents â€” the Red-tagged individual's fear of being killed or illegally abducted is therefore an 'actionable wrong' under SND v. Manalo's doctrine that the right to security exists independently of the right to liberty.
stances.rhetorical_move: third-party-violence-risk argument grounded in security-independent-of-liberty doctrine

notable_anecdotes.summary: CJ's personal recollection of asking the eminent J Azcuna how the writ came about â€” receiving the reply that it originated from Mexico, was expounded by Prof. HÃ©ctor Fix-Zamudio, and was twice carried by Azcuna into Philippine constitutional drafting (1971 ConCon failure, 1986 ConCom success after CJ Concepcion's redirection) â€” used to bestow the title 'Father of the Writ of Amparo.'

The 1991 bar exam question by Azcuna asking about the writ's intent and constitutional basis â€” 'very few answered correctly,' confirming the doctrinal obscurity of amparo even within the bar at that time.

The vivid taxonomy of expressive behaviors covered as activist freedom-of-expression: 'public rallies, writings, speeches, and body language like fist clenching, sneering, gesticulating, and staring' â€” applying constitutional protection to gestural and somatic expression.
The closing semi-joke that Zalameda's treatise is so comprehensive 'it could erupt in another bar exam' â€” connecting the column back to the 1991 bar question that opened it.

decision_framework_signals: uses writ-vs-privilege distinction as primary doctrinal lens
maps evidentiary thresholds across writ, civil, and criminal cases for comparative grounding
treats institutional-paternity (Azcuna) as legitimate basis for recognition independent of strict authorship
applies third-party-violence-risk analysis to recognize Red-tagging as an actionable threat
anchors right-to-security as an independent right separable from right-to-liberty (SND v. Manalo)
uses bar-exam relevance as proxy for doctrinal importance

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban celebrates the Supreme Court's unanimous ruling in Deduro v. Vinoya (July 4, 2023, released May 8, 2024) â€” a 39-page treatise expertly penned by Justice Rodil V. Zalameda â€” issuing the writ of amparo to a Red-tagged petitioner, calling it 'a victory of activists' from journalists and students to laborers, jeepney drivers, and plain citizens exercising freedom of expression via rallies, writings, speeches, and even body language like fist clenching, sneering, and staring. He carefully distinguishes the 'writ of amparo' (ex parte issuance on prima facie face-of-petition showing) from the 'privilege of the writ of amparo' (granted only after summary hearings on substantial evidence), analogizing to but distinguishing from preliminary vs permanent injunction in civil cases. He bestows on retired Justice Adolfo S. Azcuna the title 'Father of the Writ of Amparo in the Philippines' â€” Azcuna, as 1991 bar examiner, asked the question very few answered correctly, having earlier proposed inclusion of the writ in the 1973 Constitution (failed) and the 1986 Bill of Rights (redirected to SC powers by CJ Roberto Concepcion). He explains the writ's coverage of (a) extralegal killings, (b) enforced disappearances (per RA 10353), and (c) threats thereof â€” including, per SND v. Manalo (Oct 7, 2008), threats to security existing independently of deprivation of liberty â€” making Red-tagging a cognizable cause of action because the Red-tagged person becomes a target of vigilantes, paramilitary groups, or state agents. Deduro must still produce substantial evidence at the summary proceedings to obtain the actual privilege. CJ defers parties and process to law students because the treatise 'could erupt in another bar exam.'
